title: Values & Types
description: Define immutable values and understand static typing.

concept.definition: Haskell values are immutable and every expression has a type.
concept.analogy: A value is a sealed label; type is its category.

defaultCode:
main :: IO ()
main = do
  let name = "StepViz"
  let count = 2
  putStrLn (name ++ " " ++ show count)

title: Functions
description: Write pure functions with type signatures.

concept.definition: Functions are first-class and typically pure in Haskell.
concept.analogy: A function is a transformation pipeline from input to output.

defaultCode:
add :: Int -> Int -> Int
add a b = a + b

main :: IO ()
main = print (add 3 4)

title: Conditionals
description: Branch logic using if/then/else and guards.

concept.definition: Haskell conditionals are expressions that must return a value.
concept.analogy: Conditionals choose one expression path to evaluate.
keyPoints: Pattern matching often replaces condition chains

defaultCode:
grade :: Int -> String
grade score =
  if score >= 90 then "A"
  else if score >= 80 then "B"
  else "C"

main :: IO ()
main = putStrLn (grade 82)

title: Lists

concept.definition: Lists are homogeneous, immutable sequences in Haskell.
concept.analogy: A list is a chain where each item points to the next.

defaultCode:
main :: IO ()
main = do
  let nums = [1,2,3,4]
  print (filter even nums)

title: Pattern Matching
description: Destructure inputs directly in function definitions.

concept.definition: Pattern matching checks value shape and extracts data.
concept.analogy: Patterns are templates values can fit into.

defaultCode:
describe :: [Int] -> String
describe [] = "empty"
describe (x:_) = "starts with " ++ show x

main :: IO ()
main = putStrLn (describe [2,3])

title: Recursion
description: Solve repeated computation via base and recursive cases.

concept.definition: Recursion defines solutions in terms of smaller subproblems.
concept.analogy: Each call peels one layer until base case is reached.

defaultCode:
sumList :: [Int] -> Int
sumList [] = 0
sumList (x:xs) = x + sumList xs

main :: IO ()
main = print (sumList [1,2,3])

title: Higher-Order Functions
description: Pass and return functions for composition and reuse.

concept.definition: Higher-order functions take functions as arguments or return them.
concept.analogy: They are function combinators for building pipelines.

defaultCode:
doubleAll :: [Int] -> [Int]
doubleAll xs = map (*2) xs

main :: IO ()
main = print (doubleAll [1,2,3])

title: Type Classes
description: Define shared behavior contracts across types.

concept.definition: Type classes define polymorphic interfaces implemented by types.
concept.analogy: A type class is a capability contract for many types.

defaultCode:
isEqual :: Eq a => a -> a -> Bool
isEqual a b = a == b

main :: IO ()
main = print (isEqual 3 3)

title: Algebraic Data Types
description: Model domain states using sum and product types.

concept.definition: ADTs combine constructors to represent structured domain models.
concept.analogy: ADTs are custom blueprints for valid states.

defaultCode:
data Status = Pending | Done

label :: Status -> String
label Pending = "pending"
label Done = "done"

main :: IO ()
main = putStrLn (label Pending)

title: Maybe & Either
description: Represent optional values and typed errors safely.

concept.definition: Maybe models absence; Either models success/error outcomes.
concept.analogy: Maybe is value-or-nothing; Either is success-or-failure box.

defaultCode:
safeHead :: [a] -> Maybe a
safeHead [] = Nothing
safeHead (x:_) = Just x

main :: IO ()
main = print (safeHead [1,2,3])

title: I/O Basics
description: Work with side effects through IO actions.

concept.definition: IO actions describe side effects while preserving pure core logic.
concept.analogy: IO is a controlled boundary between pure code and the real world.
keyPoints: Keep pure logic outside IO when possible

defaultCode:
main :: IO ()
main = do
  putStrLn "Hello"
  line <- getLine
  putStrLn ("You typed: " ++ line)

title: Functors & Monads
description: Compose effectful computations with fmap and bind.

concept.definition: Functors map over wrapped values; monads sequence wrapped computations.
concept.analogy: They are abstraction layers for context-aware computation.

defaultCode:
main :: IO ()
main = do
  let x = Just 3
  print (fmap (+1) x)
